title: Chapter IX.

source.title: NPNF-211. Sulpitius Severus, Vincent of Lerins, John Cassian

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: “I will make use of another not dissimilar marvel in a like kind of work, having the concurrence of Refrigerius in doing so.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: Martin was prepared to throw down a pillar of immense size, on the top of which an idol stood, but there was no means by which effect could be given to his design.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: Well, according to his usual practice, he betakes himself to prayer.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: It is undoubted that then a column, to a certain degree like the other, rushed down from heaven, and falling upon the idol, it crushed to powder the whole of the seemingly indestructible mass: this would have been a small matter, had he only in an invisible way made use of the powers of heaven, but these very powers were beheld by human eyes serving Martin in a visible manner.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: “Again, the same Refrigerius is my witness that a woman, suffering from an issue of blood, when she had touched the garment of Martin, after the example of the woman mentioned in the Gospel, was cured in a moment of time.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: “A serpent, cutting its way through a river, was swimming towards the bank on which we had taken our stand.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: ‘In the name of the Lord,’ said Martin, ‘I command thee to return.’

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: Instantly, at the word of the holy man, the venomous beast turned round, and while we looked on, swam across to the farther bank.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: As we all perceived that this had not happened without a miracle; he groaned deeply, and exclaimed, ‘Serpents hear me, but men will not hear.’
